question: Can Qwen3 thinking-mode full-trace token-parity vs the pinned llama.cpp reference be achieved via a shared deterministic reduction-order configuration (promoting the leading-trace claim)?
verdict: NO — not achievable as a configuration. The block-level reduction order is already shared and stable; the residual divergence is fixed per-kernel f32 arithmetic (camelid decode-vs-prefill, and camelid-vs-ggml SIMD), not a reduction-order knob. Leading-trace claim retained; no semantic-agreement fallback used.

H1_mechanism.within_engine_reduction_order_noise: ~0 — both camelid --deterministic and the ggml reference are reduction-order-STABLE: ggml top-2 gaps were bit-identical under -t 1 vs -t 8, and camelid --deterministic is bit-exact across runs/threads by design (DECISIONS.md D9). So the divergence is NOT explained by within-engine reduction-order noise.
H1_mechanism.kill_condition: TRIGGERED in spirit: the perturbation that flips these near-ties is far larger than within-engine reduction-order noise (~0). It is accumulated cross-kernel f32 divergence: camelid prefill vs decode differs by ~0.71 logit on the landmarks/sites pair at capital-of-France token 73 (prefill: +0.7064 landmarks; decode: picks sites), and camelid prefill vs ggml differs by ~0.54 logit on the same pair (camelid +0.7064 vs reference +0.1656). These are real numerical differences > reduction-order noise.

two_mechanisms.dominant_decode_vs_prefill: 3 of 4 probes (capital@73, 2+2@205, hello@26): camelid PREFILL of the agreed prefix reproduces the reference argmax, but camelid incremental DECODE flips it. camelid prefill matches the reference forward pass; the autoregressive decode path diverges (different kernels GEMV-vs-GEMM + step-wise KV accumulation). This is camelid-internal.
two_mechanisms.cross_implementation: 1 of 4 probes (primary-color@27, gap 0.0218 — razor-thin): camelid PREFILL also disagrees with the reference, i.e. fixed camelid-vs-ggml kernel-arithmetic rounding across 28 layers.

deterministic_mode_finding: camelid --deterministic produces the EXACT same divergence points (73/27/205/26) as the cpu_reference path used in the shipped leading-trace bundle. The cpu_reference path was already on the order-stable CPU kernels; --deterministic does not move the cross-engine divergence. So a deterministic shared-order config does not help.
why_not_a_config: The shared block-level reduction order (per-output serial K, ascending Q8_0 blocks, --no-repack layout) is already adopted by both sides. The residual is (a) camelid decode kernels != camelid prefill kernels and (b) camelid CPU dot (i8mm/dotprod/vDSP) != ggml vec_dot — fixed per-kernel f32 arithmetic, ISA/implementation-dependent (DECISIONS.md D9 explicitly notes values are ISA-dependent). Making them argmax-identical over a full trace requires reimplementing camelid decode to bit-match its prefill AND camelid kernels to bit-match ggml — reimplementation (path a), not a shared-order configuration.
even_if_decode_equaled_prefill: Full-trace parity would STILL fail: primary-color@27 is a cross-implementation razor-tie (0.0218 logit) that flips regardless of decode/prefill unification.
actionable_engineering_lead: The dominant divergence source is camelid decode-vs-prefill numerical inconsistency (~0.7 logit by token 73, accumulated over decode steps; argmax-consistent for tokens 0..72 then flips). The reference (ggml) does not show this decode-vs-prefill flip here. Aligning camelid decode numerics to its prefill path would shrink long-generation divergence (affects all long greedy generation, not just thinking mode) — this is the real numerical discrepancy the kill condition points to.

probes.prompt: What is the capital of France?
probes.mechanism: camelid decode-vs-prefill (prefill matches reference, decode flips)
camelid decode-vs-prefill (prefill matches reference, decode flips)
camelid decode-vs-prefill (prefill matches reference, decode flips)

outcome: Leading-trace thinking-mode claim retained unchanged. No promotion. No path-(b) semantic/text-level agreement used.
